name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
۞ شَرَعَ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلدِّينِ مَا وَصَّىٰ بِهِۦ نُوحًا وَٱلَّذِىٓ أَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَيْكَ وَمَا وَصَّيْنَا بِهِۦٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَمُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰٓ ۖ أَنْ أَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلدِّينَ وَلَا تَتَفَرَّقُوا۟ فِيهِ ۚ كَبُرَ عَلَى ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ مَا تَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَيْهِ ۚ ٱللَّهُ يَجْتَبِىٓ إِلَيْهِ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَهْدِىٓ إِلَيْهِ مَن يُنِيبُ (١٣)
```

```english
He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have [also] revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus, declaring, ‘Maintain the religion, and do not be divided in it.’ Hard on the polytheists is that to which you summon them. Allah chooses for it whomever He wishes, and He guides to it whomever returns penitently [to Him]. (13)
```

```arabic
وَمَا تَفَرَّقُوٓا۟ إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْعِلْمُ بَغْيًۢا بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ وَلَوْلَا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِن رَّبِّكَ إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى لَّقُضِىَ بَيْنَهُمْ ۚ وَإِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ أُورِثُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِنۢ بَعْدِهِمْ لَفِى شَكٍّ مِّنْهُ مُرِيبٍ (١٤)
```

```english
They did not divide [into sects] except after the knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves; and were it not for a prior decree of your Lord [granting them reprieve] until a specified time, decision would have been made between them. Indeed those who were made heirs to the Book after them are in grave doubt concerning it. (14)
```

```arabic
فَلِذَٰلِكَ فَٱدْعُ ۖ وَٱسْتَقِمْ كَمَآ أُمِرْتَ ۖ وَلَا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْوَآءَهُمْ ۖ وَقُلْ ءَامَنتُ بِمَآ أَنزَلَ ٱللَّهُ مِن كِتَـٰبٍ ۖ وَأُمِرْتُ لِأَعْدِلَ بَيْنَكُمُ ۖ ٱللَّهُ رَبُّنَا وَرَبُّكُمْ ۖ لَنَآ أَعْمَـٰلُنَا وَلَكُمْ أَعْمَـٰلُكُمْ ۖ لَا حُجَّةَ بَيْنَنَا وَبَيْنَكُمُ ۖ ٱللَّهُ يَجْمَعُ بَيْنَنَا ۖ وَإِلَيْهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ (١٥)
```

```english
So summon to this [unity of religion], and be steadfast, just as you have been commanded, and do not follow their desires, and say, ‘I believe in whatever Book Allah has sent down. I have been commanded to do justice among you. Allah is our Lord and your Lord. Our deeds belong to us and your deeds belong to you. There is no quarrel between us and you. Allah will bring us together and toward Him is the destination.’ (15)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ يُحَآجُّونَ فِى ٱللَّهِ مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا ٱسْتُجِيبَ لَهُۥ حُجَّتُهُمْ دَاحِضَةٌ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ وَعَلَيْهِمْ غَضَبٌ وَلَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ (١٦)
```

```english
Those who argue concerning Allah, after His call has been answered, their argument stands refuted with their Lord, and upon them shall be [His] wrath, and there is a severe punishment for them. (16)
```

## (Commentary)

A third section of the verses defines divine revelation (الوحي الإلهي) through its effect, which is its core message and the content it encompasses—namely, the single divine religion (الدين الإلهي الواحد) that people must adopt as a way of life (سنة في الحياة) and a path traversed toward their ultimate felicity (سعادتهم).

In these verses, as necessitated by the context, it is explained that the Muhammadan law (الشريعة المحمدية) is the most comprehensive of all revealed laws. It further clarifies that the differences occurring within the religion of Allah, despite its inherent unity, do not stem from the side of heavenly revelation, but rather from the transgression (بغي) of people after knowledge had come to them. Other benefits within the verses will be pointed out during the discussion.

His saying, the Exalted: **"He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah and which We have revealed to you, and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus"** (شَرَعَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الدِّينِ ما وَصَّى بِهِ نُوحاً وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ وَما وَصَّيْنا بِهِ إِبْراهِيمَ وَمُوسى وَعِيسى). It is said: "He prescribed (شرع) the path," meaning He made it a clear and manifest way. Al-Raghib said: "The testament (الوصية) is to charge another with what is to be done, coupled with an exhortation." It is derived from their saying: "A joined land (أرض واصية)," referring to land with continuous vegetation. It is said: *awsahu* (أوصاه) and *wassahu* (وصاه) [he enjoined him]. End quote. In its meaning, there is an intimation of importance; for not every matter is enjoined as a testament (يوصى به), but rather that which the testator (الموصي) deems significant and worthy of concern is chosen for it.

Thus, His saying: **"He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah"** (شَرَعَ لَكُمْ مِنَ الدِّينِ ما وَصَّى بِهِ نُوحاً) means He has clarified and made manifest for you the religion—which is the way of life (سنة الحياة)—that He previously charged and entrusted to Noah with great concern. What is apparent from the context is that the address is to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and his community (أمته), and that what was enjoined upon Noah refers to the law (شريعة) of Noah عليه‌السلام.

His saying: **"and which We have revealed to you"** (وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ). The apparent contrast between this and the mention of Noah عليه‌السلام suggests that what was revealed to him refers to the specific knowledge (المعارف) and rulings (الأحكام) unique to his law. This was expressed through "revelation" (الإيحاء) rather than "enjoining" (التوصية) because enjoining, as mentioned, pertains specifically to matters of particular importance and concern—namely, the most essential beliefs and deeds. The law of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله is comprehensive of everything, great and small, containing both the most essential matters and others. This is in contrast to the laws of others, which were limited to the most essential matters suitable for the state of their nations and consistent with the extent of their readiness.

The shift (الالتفات) in His saying: **"and which We have revealed"** (وَالَّذِي أَوْحَيْنا) from the third person to the first person plural is to indicate grandeur (العظمة), for the great speak of themselves and their servants and followers.

His saying: **"and which We had enjoined upon Abraham, Moses and Jesus"** (وَما وَصَّيْنا بِهِ إِبْراهِيمَ وَمُوسى وَعِيسى) is coordinated with His saying: **"which He had enjoined"** (ما وَصَّى بِهِ), and it refers to what was prescribed for each of them عليه‌السلام.

The order in which they عليه‌السلام are mentioned follows their chronological sequence: Noah, then Abraham, then Moses, then Jesus عليه‌السلام. The mention of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was placed first for honor and preference, as in His saying, the Exalted: **"And when We took from the prophets their covenant, and from you and from Noah, Abraham, Moses and Jesus son of Mary"** (وَإِذْ أَخَذْنا مِنَ النَّبِيِّينَ مِيثاقَهُمْ وَمِنْكَ وَمِنْ نُوحٍ وَإِبْراهِيمَ وَمُوسى وَعِيسَى ابْنِ مَرْيَمَ) [Al-Ahzab: 7]. Noah was mentioned first among the others to indicate the antiquity of this law and its long history.

Several points can be derived from the verse:

First: Since the context implies the bestowal of favor (الامتنان), especially considering the end of the verse and the following verse, it indicates that the Muhammadan law is comprehensive (جامعة) of the previous laws. This does not contradict His saying, the Exalted: **"For every one of you We have appointed a law and a way"** (لِكُلٍّ جَعَلْنا مِنْكُمْ شِرْعَةً وَمِنْهاجاً) [Al-Ma'idah: 48], because a law being specific does not negate its comprehensiveness.

Second: The divine laws (الشرائع الإلهية) attributed to revelation are only the laws of Noah, Abraham, Moses, Jesus, and Muhammad عليه‌السلام. Had there been others, they would have been mentioned to fulfill the requirement of the aforementioned comprehensiveness.

This necessitates, firstly: that there was no law (شريعة) before Noah عليه‌السلام in the sense of laws governing human society and resolving social disputes. Some discussion on this has already passed in the commentary on His saying, the Exalted: **"Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent the prophets..."** (كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللهُ النَّبِيِّينَ) [Al-Baqarah: 213].

Secondly: The prophets sent after Noah followed his law until the mission of Abraham, and thereafter followed Abraham's law until the mission of Moses, and so on.

Third: The prophets who were possessors of laws (أصحاب الشرائع) and the "Possessors of Resolve" (أولو العزم) are these five mentioned in the verse. Had there been others with them, they would have been mentioned. These are the masters of the prophets, and their precedence is also indicated by His saying: **"And when We took from the prophets their covenant, and from you and from Noah, Abraham, Moses and Jesus son of Mary"** (وَإِذْ أَخَذْنا مِنَ النَّبِيِّينَ مِيثاقَهُمْ وَمِنْكَ وَمِنْ نُوحٍ وَإِبْراهِيمَ وَمُوسى وَعِيسَى ابْنِ مَرْيَمَ) [Al-Ahzab: 7].

His saying: **"to maintain the religion and not to be divided"** (أَنْ أَقِيمُوا الدِّينَ وَلا تَتَفَرَّقُوا). The word "that" (أَنْ) is explanatory (تفسيرية). Maintaining the religion (إقامة الدين) means preserving it through following and practice. The definite article in "the religion" (الدين) is for reference (للعهد), meaning: maintain this religion prescribed for you. Not being divided therein means preserving its unity by agreeing upon it and not differing over it.

Since prescribing the religion for them was effectively a command for all of them to follow it and act upon it without disagreement, He explained it as the command to maintain the religion and not be divided therein. The sum of it is that they are all required to maintain the religion collectively and not to be divided or scattered by some maintaining it while others abandon it. Maintaining it consists of believing in all that Allah has revealed and acting upon what is obligatory to act upon.

Thus, all the laws that Allah revealed to His prophets are one religion (دين واحد) that must be maintained without division. As for the heavenly rulings (الأحكام السماوية) that are shared and remain as long as moral obligation (التكليف) remains, the meaning of "maintaining" them is clear. As for the rulings prescribed in some of these laws that were abrogated (المنسوخة) in a subsequent law, the reality of an abrogated ruling is that it was a ruling with a specific duration for a specific group of people at a specific time. Its abrogation (نسخه) means the clarification that its duration has ended, not the appearance of its invalidity. The Exalted said: **"and Allah speaks the truth, and He guides to the way"** (وَاللهُ يَقُولُ الْحَقَّ وَهُوَ يَهْدِي السَّبِيلَ) [Al-Ahzab: 4]. Thus, the abrogated ruling is always "truth" (حق), except that it was specific to a particular group at a particular time; they were required to believe in it and act upon it, while others are required only to believe in it without acting upon it. This is the meaning of maintaining it and not being divided therein.

It becomes clear, then, that the command to maintain the religion and not be divided therein in His saying: **"to maintain the religion and not to be divided therein"** (أَنْ أَقِيمُوا الدِّينَ وَلا تَتَفَرَّقُوا فِيهِ) is absolute (مطلق), encompassing all people in all times.

By this, the error of the view held by a group becomes apparent—that the command to maintain and not divide only includes the rulings shared between the laws, excluding the specific ones, which they claim are varying rulings that differ according to the nations' conditions and interests.

This is because there is no reason to restrict the absolute nature of His saying: **"maintain the religion and not be divided therein."** If it were as they say, the command to maintain would be restricted to the three principles of religion (أصول الدين): Monotheism (التوحيد), Prophethood (النبوة), and the Resurrection (المعاد). As for other secondary rulings (الأحكام الفرعية), there is hardly a single ruling shared in all its details among all the laws. This is something that the context of His saying: **"He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined..."** etc., absolutely rejects. The same applies to His saying: **"Indeed this community of yours is one community, and I am your Lord, so be wary of Me. But they fragmented their affair among themselves into sects"** (وَإِنَّ هذِهِ أُمَّتُكُمْ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً وَأَنَا رَبُّكُمْ فَاتَّقُونِ فَتَقَطَّعُوا أَمْرَهُمْ بَيْنَهُمْ زُبُراً) [Al-Mu'minun: 52-53], and His saying: **"Indeed, with Allah religion is Islam, and those who were given the Book did not differ except after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves"** (إِنَّ الدِّينَ عِنْدَ اللهِ الْإِسْلامُ وَمَا اخْتَلَفَ الَّذِينَ أُوتُوا الْكِتابَ إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ بَغْياً بَيْنَهُمْ) [Al 'Imran: 19].

His saying: **"Hard for the polytheists is that to which you summon them"** (كَبُرَ عَلَى الْمُشْرِكِينَ ما تَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَيْهِ). What is meant by **"that to which you summon them"** is the religion of monotheism (دين التوحيد) to which the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was calling, not merely the principle of monotheism alone, as the following verse testifies. Its being "hard" (كبره) for the polytheists refers to their extreme difficulty and aversion to accepting it.

His saying: **"Allah chooses for Himself whomever He wishes and guides to Himself whoever returns"** (اللهُ يَجْتَبِي إِلَيْهِ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَيَهْدِي إِلَيْهِ مَنْ يُنِيبُ). Choosing (الاجتباء) is the act of gathering and drawing near. The requirement of the consistency of pronouns is that the second and third pronouns in **"to Himself"** (إِلَيْهِ) refer back to the same referent as the first. The meaning is: Allah gathers and draws toward the religion of monotheism—which is what you call them to—whomever He wishes of His servants, and He guides toward it whoever returns to Him. Thus, the entirety of His saying: **"Hard for the polytheists is that to which you summon them. Allah chooses for Himself whomever He wishes"** carries the same meaning as His saying: **"He has chosen you and has not placed for you any hardship in religion, the faith of your father, Abraham"** (هُوَ اجْتَباكُمْ وَما جَعَلَ عَلَيْكُمْ فِي الدِّينِ مِنْ حَرَجٍ مِلَّةَ أَبِيكُمْ إِبْراهِيمَ) [Al-Hajj: 78].

It has been said that the two pronouns refer to Allah Himself, and there is no harm in this, but the previous interpretation is more suitable. In any case, His saying: **"Allah chooses for Himself..."** to the end of the verse is placed in a position of dispensing with the faith of the arrogant polytheists, similar to His saying, the Exalted: **"But if they are disdainful, those who are with your Lord glorify Him night and day, and they do not tire"** (فإِنِ اسْتَكْبَرُوا فَالَّذِينَ عِنْدَ رَبِّكَ يُسَبِّحُونَ لَهُ بِاللَّيْلِ وَالنَّهارِ وَهُمْ لا يَسْأَمُونَ) [Fussilat: 38].

It has also been said that what is meant by **"that to which you summon them"** is that which you call them to believe in, which is the Message (الرسالة)—meaning your message has become hard for them. And His saying: **"Allah chooses..."** etc., would be in the sense of His saying: **"Allah knows best where to place His message"** (اللهُ أَعْلَمُ حَيْثُ يَجْعَلُ رِسالَتَهُ) [Al-An'am: 124], but this is contrary to the apparent meaning.

His saying, the Exalted: **"They did not divide except after knowledge had come to them, out of envy among themselves"** (وَما تَفَرَّقُوا إِلَّا مِنْ بَعْدِ ما جاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ بَغْياً بَيْنَهُمْ) to the end of the verse. The pronoun in **"they divided"** (تَفَرَّقُوا) refers to the people understood from the context. Transgression (البغي) is injustice or envy, and its qualification with **"among themselves"** (بَيْنَهُمْ) indicates its mutual occurrence. The meaning is: the people for whom the law was prescribed did not divide through their disagreement and abandonment of unity except while their division was stemming—or originating—from after knowledge of the truth had come to them, out of injustice or envy that they practiced among themselves.

This is the disagreement in religion (الاختلاف في الدين) that leads to sects and factions, which Allah, the Glorified, attributes in various places in His speech to transgression (البغي). As for the disagreement that leads to the revelation of law—which is the disagreement in the affairs of life and division in matters of livelihood—it is a matter returning to the variation of human natures in their goals. It is the pretext for the descent of revelation and the prescription of law to resolve it, as indicated by His saying: **"Mankind were a single community; then Allah sent the prophets..."** (كانَ النَّاسُ أُمَّةً واحِدَةً فَبَعَثَ اللهُ النَّبِيِّينَ) [Al-Baqarah: 213], as previously explained.

His saying: **"And were it not for a prior word from your Lord [postponing them] until a stated term, it would have been decided between them"** (وَلَوْ لا كَلِمَةٌ سَبَقَتْ مِنْ رَبِّكَ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى لَقُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ). The "word" (الكلمة) refers to something like His saying at the time of Adam's عليه‌السلام descent to the earth: **"and for you there is an abode on the earth and a provision for a while"** (وَلَكُمْ فِي الْأَرْضِ مُسْتَقَرٌّ وَمتاعٌ إِلى حِينٍ) [Al-Baqarah: 36].

The meaning is: Were it not that Allah had decreed for them settlement and enjoyment on the earth until a term He named and designated, it would have been decided between them immediately following their division in His religion and their deviation from His path, and He would have destroyed them as demanded by this grave sin.

The objection of one who says: "Allah has indeed decided and destroyed, as He narrates in the stories of Noah, Hud, and Salih عليه‌السلام, and He has said: **'There is a messenger for every nation: so when their messenger comes, it is decided between them with justice'** (وَلِكُلِّ أُمَّةٍ رَسُولٌ فَإِذا جاءَ رَسُولُهمْ قُضِيَ بَيْنَهُمْ بِالْقِسْطِ) [Yunus: 47]"—

This is refuted by the fact that the judgment and destruction narrated by the Exalted occurred only among the nations of the prophets during their own time, among the deniers who rejected them. What we are concerned with here in His saying: **"And were it not for a prior word from your Lord..."** is regarding their nations after them, which is clear from the context.

His saying: **"Indeed those who were made to inherit the Book after them are in a disquieting doubt about it"** (وَإِنَّ الَّذِينَ أورِثُوا الْكِتابَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ لَفِي شَكٍّ مِنْهُ مُرِيبٍ). The pronoun in **"after them"** (مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ) refers to those who divided after knowledge out of envy among themselves—the predecessors (الأسلاف). Those who were made to inherit the Book after them are their successors (أخلافهم). The meaning of the verse is that those who initiated the disagreement and established the division did so while having knowledge of the truth, and they only innovated what they innovated out of envy among themselves. Their successors, who inherited the Book after them, are in a disquieting doubt (شك مريب)—one that causes suspicion—concerning it.

What we have presented regarding the meaning of the verse is what the context provides. Others have many opinions in its interpretation that are of no use to recount; one may refer to their books to become acquainted with them.

His saying, the Exalted: **"So summon to that, and be steadfast as you have been commanded, and do not follow their desires"** (فَلِذلِكَ فَادْعُ وَاسْتَقِمْ كَما أُمِرْتَ وَلا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْواءَهُمْ) to the end of the verse. This is a derivation (تفريع) from what was mentioned regarding the prescription of one religion for all prophets and their nations, and then the division of their nations into predecessors who differed in religion despite knowledge out of envy, and successors who are doubting and suspicious of the Book they inherited. That is: because He has prescribed for you all that He prescribed for those before you, then summon [to it]; and because of what was mentioned regarding the division of some out of envy and the suspicion of others, then be steadfast as you have been commanded and do not follow their desires.

The "for" (اللام) in His saying: **"So for that"** (فَلِذلِكَ) is for causation (للتعليل). It has been said that the *lam* means "to" (إلى), i.e., "To that which has been prescribed for you of religion, summon and be steadfast as you have been commanded." Steadfastness (الاستقامة)—as mentioned by Al-Raghib—is to adhere to the straight path (المنهاج المستقيم). His saying: **"and do not follow their desires"** (وَلا تَتَّبِعْ أَهْواءَهُمْ) is like an explanation for it.

His saying: **"and say, 'I believe in whatever Book Allah has sent down'"** (وَقُلْ آمَنْتُ بِما أَنْزَلَ اللهُ مِنْ كِتابٍ) is an equalization between the heavenly books in terms of confirming them and believing in them; these are the books revealed from Allah containing the laws.

His saying: **"and I have been commanded to do justice among you"** (وَأُمِرْتُ لِأَعْدِلَ بَيْنَكُمُ). It has been said that the *lam* is extra for emphasis, similar to His saying: **"and we have been commanded to submit to the Lord of all the worlds"** (وَأُمِرْنا لِنُسْلِمَ لِرَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ) [Al-An'am: 71]. The meaning is: "I have been commanded to do justice among you," i.e., to treat you equally, so I do not prefer the strong over the weak, nor the rich over the poor, nor the great over the small; nor do I favor the white over the black, nor the Arab over the non-Arab, nor a Hashimite or Qurayshite over another. The call is directed to everyone, and people are equal before the divine law.

Thus, His saying: **"I believe in whatever Book Allah has sent down"** is an equalization between the revealed books in terms of belief in them, and His saying: **"and I have been commanded to do justice among you"** is an equalization between people in terms of the call and the application of the law he brought.

It has also been said that the *lam* in **"to do justice among you"** (لِأَعْدِلَ بَيْنَكُمُ) is for causation, meaning: "I have been commanded with what I have been commanded for the purpose of doing justice among you." Similarly, it has been said that justice (العدل) refers to justice in ruling, or justice in judging between you, or other things. These are remote meanings not supported by the context.

His saying: **"Allah is our Lord and your Lord"** (اللهُ رَبُّنا وَرَبُّكُمْ) etc., is in the position of providing the reason (التعليل) for the aforementioned equalization between the books and laws in believing in them, and between people in their call and the inclusiveness of the rulings for them. Therefore, the speech was presented with a break (فصل) without a coordinating conjunction.

So His saying: **"Allah is our Lord and your Lord"** points out that the Lord of all is the one Allah, the Exalted. They do not have many lords such that each could be attached to his own lord and they would excel one another by their lords, or each would restrict himself to believing in the law of his own lord. Rather, Allah is the Lord of all, and they are all His servants, owned by Him and governed by His command. The laws revealed to the prophets are from Him, so there is no reason to believe in some and not others—as the Jews believe in the law of Moses but not those after him, and similarly the Christians in the law of Jesus but not Muhammad صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. Rather, it is obligatory to believe in every Book revealed from Him because they are all from Him.

His saying: **"To us our deeds and to you your deeds"** (لَنا أَعْمالُنا وَلَكُمْ أَعْمالُكُمْ) points out that deeds, though they differ in being good or evil and in their recompense of reward or punishment, do not transcend their doer. Every person has what he has earned; no one benefits from the deed of another, nor is anyone harmed by the deed of another. Thus, he has no right to advance a person to benefit from his deed or delay a person to be harmed by his deed. Yes, in deeds there is a hierarchy by which the degrees of the doers differ, but that is for Allah in how He calls His servants to account, not for the people—the Prophet and those below him—who are all owned servants, none of whom possesses anything for another. This is what the Exalted mentioned in the dialogue of Noah عليه‌السلام with his people: **"They said, 'Shall we believe in you when it is the lowliest who follow you?' He said, 'What knowledge do I have of what they used to do? Their reckoning is only with my Lord, if you only perceived'"** (قالُوا أَنُؤْمِنُ لَكَ وَاتَّبَعَكَ الْأَرْذَلُونَ قالَ وَما عِلْمِي بِما كانُوا يَعْمَلُونَ إِنْ حِسابُهُمْ إِلَّا عَلى رَبِّي لَوْ تَشْعُرُونَ) [Al-Shu'ara: 111-113]. Similarly, His saying addressing the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: **"You are not responsible for any part of their reckoning, nor are they responsible for any part of your reckoning"** (ما عَلَيْكَ مِنْ حِسابِهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ وَما مِنْ حِسابِكَ عَلَيْهِمْ مِنْ شَيْءٍ) [Al-An'am: 52].

His saying: **"There is no argument between us and you"** (لا حُجَّةَ بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَكُمُ). Perhaps the meaning is that there is no argument (حجة) indicating the precedence of some over others between us, which some could establish over others to prove their superiority.

It is possible that the negation of argument is a metaphor for the negation of its consequence, which is contention (الخصومة). That is: there is no contention between us regarding the variation of degrees, because our Lord is one, and in the fact that we are all His servants, we are one, and every soul has what it has earned. Thus, there is no argument in the matter—meaning no contention for which an argument would be taken up.

From here, it becomes clear that there is no basis for the view of some in interpreting the sentence as: "meaning no disputation or contention because the truth has become manifest, so there remains no need for disputation nor any ground for opposition except for obstinacy and stubbornness." End quote. This is because the speech is directed toward explaining what the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله was commanded with regarding himself and his community of the way of equality (سنة التسوية), not to prove any of the principles of knowledge (أصول المعارف) such that "argument" would be interpreted as he interpreted it.

His saying: **"Allah will gather us together"** (اللهُ يَجْمَعُ بَيْنَنا). The first-person plural pronoun therein refers to the collective of the speaker and the addressed in the previous sentences. The "gathering" refers to Allah's gathering them on the Day of Resurrection for reckoning and recompense, as has been said.

It is not far-fetched that the gathering refers to Allah's gathering them in Lordship (الربوبية); for He is the Lord of all and all are His servants. Thus, His saying: **"Allah will gather us together"** would be an emphasis of His previous saying: **"Allah is our Lord and your Lord,"** and a preparation and prelude for His saying: **"and to Him is the ultimate return"** (وَإِلَيْهِ الْمَصِيرُ). The meaning of the two sentences would be that Allah is our Originator because He is the Lord of us all, and to Him is our end because to Him is the ultimate return; so there is no originator for what is between us except Him, exalted is His Name.

The apparent requirement of the context for the reasoning (التعليل) would have been to say: "Allah is my Lord and your Lord, for me is my deed and for you are your deeds, there is no argument between me and you," in alignment with his saying: **"I believe"** (آمَنْتُ) and **"I have been commanded to do justice"** (وَأُمِرْتُ لِأَعْدِلَ). However, he shifted from the first-person singular to the first-person plural because his previous saying: **"He has prescribed for you the religion which He had enjoined upon Noah..."** etc., and his saying: **"Allah chooses for Himself whomever He wishes and guides to Himself whoever returns,"** indicate that there are people who believe in what the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله believed in, respond to his call, and follow his law.

Thus, the referent of the first-person plural in **"our Lord"** (رَبُّنا), **"to us our deeds"** (لَنا أَعْمالُنا), and **"between us"** (بَيْنَنا) is he صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the believers in him. The referent of the addressed in His saying: **"and your Lord"** (وَرَبُّكُمْ), **"your deeds"** (أَعْمالُكُمْ), and **"between you"** (بَيْنَكُمُ) is the rest of the people from the People of the Book and the polytheists. The verse is on the pattern of His saying, the Exalted: **"Say, 'O People of the Book, come to a word common between us and you: that we will worship no one but Allah, and that we will not ascribe any partner to Him, and that none of us will take others as lords besides Allah.' But if they turn away, say, 'Be witnesses that we are Muslims'"** (قُلْ يا أَهْلَ الْكِتابِ تَعالَوْا إِلى كَلِمَةٍ سَواءٍ بَيْنَنا وَبَيْنَكُمْ أَلَّا نَعْبُدَ إِلَّا اللهَ وَلا نُشْرِكَ بِهِ شَيْئاً وَلا يَتَّخِذَ بَعْضُنا بَعْضاً أَرْباباً مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ فَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَقُولُوا اشْهَدُوا بِأَنَّا مُسْلِمُونَ) [Al 'Imran: 64].

His saying, the Exalted: **"As for those who argue concerning Allah after He has been acknowledged, their argument is void with their Lord, and upon them shall be wrath, and for them there is a severe punishment"** (وَالَّذِينَ يُحَاجُّونَ فِي اللهِ مِنْ بَعْدِ ما اسْتُجِيبَ لَهُ حُجَّتُهُمْ داحِضَةٌ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ وَعَلَيْهِمْ غَضَبٌ وَلَهُمْ عَذابٌ شَدِيدٌ). An argument (الحجة) is the speech intended to prove or invalidate something, from *hajj* (الحج) meaning purpose. *Dahd* (الدحض) is invalidity and vanishing.

The meaning—as has been said—is: those who argue concerning Allah, i.e., they argue to negate His Lordship or to invalidate His religion after the people have responded (استجاب) to Him and entered His religion due to the manifestation of the proof and the clarity of the path—their argument is void and vanishing with their Lord, and upon them is wrath from Allah, the Exalted, and for them is a severe punishment.

What is apparent is that the "response" (الاستجابة) to Him refers to the true response, which is acceptance based on knowledge untainted by doubt, to which the sound human primordial nature (الفطرة) is compelled. For religion, with its inherent knowledge, is primordial (فطري); the living primordial nature confirms it and responds to it. The Exalted said: **"Only those who listen will respond; as for the dead, Allah will resurrect them"** (إِنَّما يَسْتَجِيبُ الَّذِينَ يَسْمَعُونَ وَالْمَوْتى يَبْعَثُهُمُ اللهُ) [Al-An'am: 36]. And He said: **"by the soul and Him who fashioned it, and inspired it with [the discernment of] its vices and its godwariness"** (وَنَفْسٍ وَما سَوَّاها فَأَلْهَمَها فُجُورَها وَتَقْواها) [Al-Shams: 7-8]. And He said: **"So set your face to the religion as a Hanif—the primordial nature of Allah according to which He originated mankind"** (فَأَقِمْ وَجْهَكَ لِلدِّينِ حَنِيفاً فِطْرَتَ اللهِ الَّتِي فَطَرَ النَّاسَ عَلَيْها) [Al-Rum: 30].

The sum of the verse, according to this, is that those who argue concerning Him, the Exalted, or concerning His religion after the response of the sound primordial nature to it, or after the response of the people through their sound primordial nature to it—their argument is void and vanishing with their Lord, and upon them is wrath from Him, and for them is a severe punishment whose magnitude cannot be measured.

This view is somewhat supported by the context of the previous verses, where it is mentioned that Allah prescribed a religion, enjoined it upon His prophets, and chose for it whomever He wished of His servants. Thus, arguing that Allah has a religion by which He makes His servants worship Him is void. It is possible then that His saying: **"It is Allah who has sent down the Book with the truth and the Balance"** (اللهُ الَّذِي أَنْزَلَ الْكِتابَ بِالْحَقِّ وَالْمِيزانَ) is in the position of providing the reason and an argument that voids their argument; so reflect upon it.

It has been said that the pronoun in **"concerning Allah"** (فِي اللهِ) refers to the Messenger صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and those who responded are the People of the Book; their response to him was their acknowledgment of the arrival of his descriptions and traits in their books. The meaning would be that their arguing concerning him after their acknowledgment of what they acknowledged is void with their Lord.

It has also been said that the pronoun refers to him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and the one who responded is Allah, the Exalted, as He responded to his prayer against the leaders of the Quraysh and killed them on the day of Badr, and his prayer against the people of Mecca so He afflicted them with drought and hardship, and his prayer for the oppressed until Allah delivered them from the hands of the Quraysh, among other miracles. Both meanings are remote from the context.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *Ruh al-Ma'ani*, regarding His saying, the Exalted: **"As for those who argue concerning Allah..."** the verse: Narrated from Ibn Abbas and Mujahid: It was revealed concerning a group of the Children of Israel—who intended to turn people away from Islam and mislead them—so they said: "Our Book is before your Book and our Prophet is before your Prophet—so our religion is better than your religion." In another narration: instead of "so our religion" etc., "so we are more entitled to Allah than you."

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ibn al-Mundhir narrated from 'Ikrimah who said: When **"When the help of Allah comes and the victory"** (إِذا جاءَ نَصْرُ اللهِ وَالْفَتْحُ) was revealed, the polytheists in Mecca said to the believers among them: "The people have entered the religion of Allah in throngs—so leave from among us—for what reason do you remain among us?" Then was revealed: **"As for those who argue concerning Allah after He has been acknowledged..."** the verse.

I say: The content of the verse does not correspond to the narration, as there is no "arguing" (محاجة) in the story. Similarly, the previous report does not suffice to explain His saying: **"after He has been acknowledged"** (مِنْ بَعْدِ ما اسْتُجِيبَ لَهُ).
